title: The Train Conductor's Routine
summary: A long-distance train conductor in Berlin describes his early-morning routine, the small habits that keep him calm, and why he still loves the job after eighteen years.
body: My day starts at four o'clock in the morning. I have been a train conductor in Berlin for eighteen years, and I still set two alarms. I drink one strong coffee at home, then walk to the depot. The walk takes twelve minutes. I always take the same route, even when it rains. I think small habits keep my mind quiet before a long day. At the depot, I check my paperwork, the schedule, and the weather report. The morning intercity to Hamburg leaves at six twenty, and I cannot be late. On the platform, I greet passengers and answer the usual questions: 'Is this the right train?' 'Where is coach number nine?' 'Can I open the window?' Most people are still sleepy and very polite. I have learned not to take rude moments personally; people are often tired or worried about something else. The best part of the job is the moment when the train begins to move and the city slides past the window. After all these years, that small motion still feels like a quiet kind of magic. When younger colleagues ask me if the work has become boring, I tell them: a job becomes boring only when you forget to notice it.

annotations.meaningVi: nhân viên soát vé / trưởng tàu
annotations.exampleEn: The train conductor checked everyone's ticket.
On exam day, I set two alarms.
He walks to the depot every morning.
She drives the same route to school every day.
Please check the schedule before you go.
He listens to the weather report every morning.
The intercity to Hamburg leaves at six twenty.
Your seat is in coach number nine.

annotations.meaningVi: để bụng những lúc người khác thô lỗ
annotations.exampleEn: Try not to take rude moments personally.
The countryside slides past the window.

annotations.meaningVi: một thứ phép thuật nhẹ nhàng
annotations.exampleEn: Snow at night has a quiet kind of magic.

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for life-long career length
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng have/has been + danh ngữ + 'for' để diễn tả khoảng thời gian gắn với một vai trò vẫn tiếp diễn. Trong bài: 'I have been a train conductor in Berlin for eighteen years.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'have/has been + noun + for + time' to express duration of a role still ongoing. From the text: 'I have been a train conductor in Berlin for eighteen years.'

grammarNotes.title: Adverbs of frequency: always, often, still
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trạng từ tần suất ('always', 'still', 'often') thường đứng trước động từ thường. Trong bài: 'I always take the same route', 'I still set two alarms', 'that small motion still feels like a quiet kind of magic.'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Adverbs of frequency ('always', 'still', 'often') usually come before the main verb. From the text: 'I always take the same route' and 'I still set two alarms'.

translationVi: Ngày của tôi bắt đầu lúc bốn giờ sáng. Tôi làm trưởng tàu ở Berlin đã mười tám năm, vậy mà vẫn đặt hai cái báo thức. Tôi uống một tách cà phê đậm ở nhà rồi đi bộ đến trạm điều hành. Quãng đường mất mười hai phút. Tôi luôn đi đúng tuyến cũ, kể cả khi trời mưa. Tôi nghĩ những thói quen nhỏ giúp đầu óc bình tĩnh trước một ngày dài. Đến nơi, tôi kiểm tra giấy tờ, lịch trình và bản tin thời tiết. Tàu liên thành phố buổi sáng đi Hamburg rời ga lúc sáu giờ hai mươi, và tôi không thể trễ. Trên sân ga, tôi chào hành khách và trả lời những câu quen thuộc: 'Đây có phải tàu đúng không?' 'Toa số chín ở đâu?' 'Tôi mở cửa sổ được không?' Phần lớn người ta vẫn còn ngái ngủ và rất lịch sự. Tôi học được cách không để bụng những lúc người ta thô lỗ; thường là họ mệt hoặc đang lo chuyện khác. Phần tôi thích nhất là khoảnh khắc tàu bắt đầu chuyển bánh và thành phố trượt qua khung cửa sổ. Sau từng ấy năm, cú dịch chuyển nhỏ ấy vẫn giống như một thứ phép thuật nhẹ nhàng. Khi các đồng nghiệp trẻ hỏi tôi rằng công việc đã trở nên nhàm chán chưa, tôi đáp: một công việc chỉ trở nên nhàm chán khi ta quên để ý đến nó.

questions.prompt: What time does the conductor's day start?
questions.explanation: 'My day starts at four o'clock in the morning.'
questions.hint: Listen for the very first sentence.

questions.prompt: How long has he worked as a conductor in Berlin?
questions.explanation: 'I have been a train conductor in Berlin for eighteen years.'
questions.hint: Listen for the number of years.

questions.prompt: Why does he still set two alarms?
options: Because he is afraid of being late
Because the depot calls him at four
questions.explanation: He says 'I still set two alarms' and later 'I cannot be late' for the train.
questions.hint: Listen for the reason behind being careful about time.

questions.prompt: How long does the walk to the depot take?
questions.hint: Listen for a specific number of minutes.

questions.prompt: Why does he always take the same route?
options: Small habits keep his mind quiet
questions.explanation: 'small habits keep my mind quiet before a long day.'
questions.hint: Listen for a reason about his mind.

questions.prompt: At what time does the morning intercity to Hamburg leave?
questions.explanation: 'The morning intercity to Hamburg leaves at six twenty.'
questions.hint: Listen for an exact departure time.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of the usual passenger questions he hears?
options: Can I have a free ticket?
questions.explanation: He lists three questions; 'free ticket' is not among them.
questions.hint: Listen for the three questions in a row.

questions.prompt: What has he learned about rude moments at work?
options: Not to take them personally, because people are often tired or worried
To report each one to the manager
questions.explanation: 'I have learned not to take rude moments personally; people are often tired or worried about something else.'
questions.hint: Listen for what he has learned over the years.

questions.prompt: What is his favourite moment in the job?
options: The moment the train starts moving and the city slides past the window
questions.explanation: 'The best part of the job is the moment when the train begins to move and the city slides past the window.'

questions.prompt: How does he describe that small motion?
questions.explanation: 'that small motion still feels like a quiet kind of magic.'

questions.prompt: What does he tell younger colleagues?
options: All jobs become boring with time
A job becomes boring only when you forget to notice it
The pay is the most important thing
questions.explanation: 'a job becomes boring only when you forget to notice it.'

blanks.explanation: 'personally' — 'take it personally' means to feel attacked.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The conductor says a job becomes boring only when you forget to notice it. Is this true for all kinds of work, or only for some? What helps a person 'notice' their own day, even when the tasks are routine?
